text: Traction sutures are placed in the anterior table of the sternum and a redo median stenotomy is performed.
Note the use of a curved saw blade which is only moved vertically when cutting the posterior table of the sternum.
Having divided the sternum, the adhesions are taken down, starting with the side opposite the ventricular mass.
In this case, the patient's right
Having already mobilised the right side, the left side can be retracted more easily allowing the plane between the ventricle and the sternum to be dissected.
Note that the cautery is being used to cut the tissue rather than to blunt dissect.
This is because the adhesions which require division are stronger than the underlying myocardium.

Once the adhesions between the heart and the chest wall have been freed, the spreader can be inserted and the aorta positioned anterior and to the right is identified first so that an arterial cannula could be inserted rapidly if an inadvertent hole is made during later dissection.
Attention is now turned to the diaphragmatic surface of the heart, the area that usually has the least dense adhesions.
Having made a good plane on the diaphragm, the right atrium is dissected.
This is often more densely adherent than in other areas.
Scissors are often extremely effective in this area and provision of counter traction is essential during dissection.
As the pericardial edge is freed, stay sutures are placed to provide traction and exposure.
The dissection is confined to only those structures required for either the anastomosis or extracardiac Fontan pathway.
A good exposure of the IVC is clearly essential.
Here the pulmonary artery is dissected from the underside of the aortic arch.
Heparin is given and allowed to circulate and a sample is drawn to check the ACT.
A purse string is placed in the right atrial appendage for both venous cannulation and if suitable for the fenestration.
Two aortic purse strings are placed.
The aortic adventitra is grasped longitudinally with forceps to raise a fold of tissue.
This is cut to make a flap using an 11 blade.
The flap is then released and grasped with the forceps transversely taking care to place one jaw of the forceps under the flap.
The aorta is then cut transversely with the 11 blade and the arteriotomy is held closed with the flap.
It is then dilated with a mosquito and the cannula is inserted.
If the cannula will not pass easily the dilatation is repeated.
Localised dissection at the aortic cannulation site is more likely to happen during a redo operation and is best avoided.
A sheath has been placed in the femoral vein percutaneously by Dr Bulls, the anaesthesiologist.
This is then used to insert the IVC cannula using the Seldinger technique.
The cannula is secured using a U-stitch which is snared and the tourniquet tied to the cannula.
The stent and position of the left SVC means that it will be easiest to perform the operation using short periods of circulatory arrest.
Bypass is started and the patient is cooled to 18 degrees Celsius.
Ice packs are placed on the head.
During cooling the heart fibrillated at which point the vent was inserted via the right atrial appendage.
It defibrillated after venting and then became asystolic as cooling progressed.
The UTI dilator is used to assess the best size of the conduit.
An 18mm Gore-Tex tube is selected.
The circulation is then arrested and the pulmonary artery is opened longitudinally.
A six-hoe proline suture is passed into out at the toe of the arteriotomy and secured with a rubber shod.
The conduit is then bevelled slightly and the anastomosis is started on the inferior wall using the other end of the suture.
The excess conduit is trimmed off to allow the conduit to be brought down into the field.
No attempt is made at this point to accurately judge the length of the conduit.
The conduit material is quite thick and can be safely used as a fulcrum to pivot the needle which is necessary here as the access under the PA is severely restricted.
Once the first quarter of the inferior wall is completed, the suture line allows a straightforward forehand approach.
Once the heel of the anastomosis is reached, the ends of the suture are switched and the superior suture line is made.
Once complete the suture is tied and bypass is restarted.
The venous return from the left glen is captured using a sucker in the conduit.
After five minutes of reperfusion the circulation is arrested.
The aorta is clamped and the IVC is disconnected leaving a cuff of right atrial free wall.
taking care to keep clear of the AV groove and coronary sinus on the medial side.
A vascular clamp is applied to the IVC stump and bypass is restarted.
The right atrium is closed with 5-O proline in two layers, again taking care to keep sutures away from the RCA and the AV groove.
Having closed the atrium, the circulation is arrested again, the cross clamp is released and the conduit is cut to length.
It is important not to leave the conduit too long in order to prevent kinking.
The anastomosis is done in the standard fashion beginning by passing the suture into out of the left hand structure, the cortex in this case, halfway down the far wall.
This is then rubber shod and the other end of the suture is used back hand into out on the IBC and then out to in on the cortex.
The other end of the suture is then taken up allowing forehand suturing starting out to in on the IVC.
Once the zenith is reached, the same needle path is achieved going backhand down the near wall.
The suture is tied and bypass is restarted.
Now all of the venous return can be captured by the IVC cannula.
Attention is now turned to the fenestration.
A side biting clamp is applied to the conduit adjacent to the chosen site.
A cruciate incision is made in the Gore-Tex which is then used to introduce a 4mm coronary punch.
Another side biting clamp is applied to the atrial appendage but the position does not look very good for this fenestration.
Instead a slight in the free wall is chosen and a second clamp is applied there.
The atrium is opened longitudinally and then sutured to the conduit so that the atrial orifice surrounds the fenestration.
The aortic cross clamp is applied briefly and the conduit is clamped at the diaphragm to allow it to fill with blood from the SVC territory.
Both side clamps are removed and the lateral part of the suture line is completed and de-aired through the suture line simultaneously.
All of the clamps are then removed and the patient is re-warmed.
Following full re-warming she was weaned from cardiopulmonary bypass.
Post-operative recovery was unremarkable and a year and a half later she continues to do well.
